summary:
The War of the Polish Succession (1733–1735 AD) was a brief European conflict triggered by competing claims to the Polish throne following the death of Augustus II — primarily a proxy struggle between France (supporting Stanisław Leszczyński, father-in-law of Louis XV) and Russia and Austria (supporting the Saxon candidate Augustus III). Despite its name suggesting a Polish focus, the war's decisive action took place primarily in Italy, where French forces fought Austria, and on the Rhine, where France recovered the Duchy of Lorraine. Poland itself was largely a spectator to its own succession crisis — the Polish nobility elected Leszczyński, but Russian military intervention quickly expelled him and installed Augustus III.

The War of the Polish Succession's outcome illustrated both the [[event:2390|Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth's]] fundamental weakness (dependent on great-power consensus for its dynastic arrangements) and the territorial reshaping of 18th-century Europe through dynastic settlement. The Peace of Vienna (1738) confirmed Augustus III as king of Poland, gave Lorraine to Leszczyński for his lifetime (reverting to France on his death), and confirmed Austria's possession of Parma while France received Naples and Sicily — territorial rearrangements negotiated in Paris and Vienna with minimal Polish input. The conflict was a rehearsal for the [[event:2334|partition of Poland]] later in the century, demonstrating that the great powers regarded Poland as a negotiating chip in European power politics rather than as a sovereign state whose wishes mattered.
